title: Cornish pasty
subtitle: Beef skirt, swede, potato, and onion in shortcrust pastry
excerpt: The traditional Cornish pasty: beef skirt, raw swede, raw potato, and onion layered inside shortcrust pastry, crimped along the top, and baked. The filling is raw when it goes in; the steam inside the sealed pastry cooks everything through. No gravy, no other additions — this is the statutory Cornish Pasty Association recipe.
sourceNotes: The Cornish pasty has Protected Geographical Indication status in the EU and UK. The official recipe specifies beef skirt, swede (not turnip), potato, onion, salt, and pepper, in shortcrust pastry with the crimp along the top. The pasty was a working lunch for Cornish tin miners — the thick crimped edge served as a handle that could be discarded, keeping dirty fingers away from the food.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked. Bake from frozen at 180°C for 55 to 60 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: Double or triple the recipe — the assembly is the same work regardless of quantity.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble and refrigerate unbaked for up to 24 hours. Bake from cold, adding 5 minutes.

content.text: The filling must be layered raw, not pre-cooked: swede on the bottom (it takes longest), then potato, then beef, then onion. Everything cooks inside the sealed pastry case in its own steam. Season generously between each layer — raw vegetables and unseasoned beef inside sealed pastry will be bland without proper seasoning at this stage.

Beef skirt is the traditional cut. It has good flavour and breaks down to tenderness during the 45-minute bake without drying out. Chuck steak is an acceptable substitute. Do not use a lean cut — it will turn dry.

items.prepNote: or beef skirt, cut into small cubes

content.text: Rub the cold {{unsalted-butter}} and {{lard}} into the {{plain-flour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Add 8 to 10 tablespoons of cold water, mixing until the dough just comes together. Do not overwork. Wrap in cling film and chill for 30 minutes.

Heat the oven to 200°C. Divide the pastry into 4 equal pieces. Roll each into a circle approximately 25 cm across. Layer the filling on one half of each circle: start with {{swede}}, then {{potato}}, then {{beef-chuck}}, then {{onion}}. Season each layer with {{sea-salt-fine}} and {{black-pepper}}. Fold the other half of the pastry over. Press the edges together firmly. Crimp: fold the edge over itself in a rope pattern along the top. Brush with beaten {{eggs}}. Bake for 45 minutes until deep golden. Allow to cool for 5 minutes before eating — the filling retains heat.

items.symptom: Pastry cracking and leaking during baking
items.cause: Crimping not tight enough, or pastry too thin at the edges
items.fix: The crimp must form a firm seal all the way around. Press hard and fold tightly — the internal steam will find any weak point and force it open

items.symptom: Filling still slightly hard in the centre
items.cause: Slices cut too thick, or oven temperature too low
items.fix: Slice the swede and potato to 3 to 4 mm thickness, no thicker. If in doubt, go thinner

items.cause: Seasoning not distributed through all layers
items.fix: Season each layer individually as you build. A single seasoning on top of the assembled pile will not penetrate to the bottom layers

content.text: The Cornish pasty is one of the most regionally specific dishes in Britain. It is a lunchtime food and a portable food, eaten by hand without cutlery. Outside Cornwall it appears at food markets, bakeries, and petrol station forecourts across Britain. The homemade version has a different texture and proportion of filling to pastry than the commercial one.
